description.task: Create indexes on a large data simulation and use EXPLAIN to see how the query planner uses them.
description.context: An index is a data structure (typically a B-tree) that the database maintains alongside a table to speed up lookups. Think of it as a book's index: instead of scanning every page, you jump directly to the right location.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
  email        VARCHAR(80),
  last_name    VARCHAR(50),
  country      VARCHAR(40),
  signup_date  DATE
);

INSERT INTO customers VALUES
  (1,  'alice@mail.com',   'Smith',    'USA', '2024-01-15'),
  (2,  'bob@mail.com',     'Jones',    'UK',  '2024-02-20'),
  (3,  'carol@mail.com',  'Williams', 'USA', '2024-03-10'),
  (4,  'dave@mail.com',   'Brown',    'CA',  '2024-04-05'),
  (5,  'eve@mail.com',    'Davis',    'USA', '2024-05-22'),
  (6,  'frank@mail.com',  'Miller',   'AU',  '2024-06-11'),
  (7,  'grace@mail.com',  'Wilson',   'USA', '2024-07-30'),
  (8,  'henry@mail.com',  'Moore',    'UK',  '2024-08-14'),
  (9,  'iris@mail.com',   'Taylor',   'USA', '2024-09-01'),
  (10, 'jack@mail.com',   'Anderson', 'CA',  '2024-10-19');

-- Create an index on email for fast lookups
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_email ON customers(email);

-- Create an index on country for filtering
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_country ON customers(country);

-- Query 1: Lookup by email (index should be used)
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE email = 'carol@mail.com';

-- Query 2: Filter by country
SELECT customer_id, last_name, country FROM customers WHERE country = 'USA' ORDER BY last_name;

-- View existing indexes on the table (PostgreSQL)
SELECT indexname, indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'customers';
instructions: Run CREATE TABLE, INSERT, and CREATE INDEX statements.
Run the pg_indexes query to list all indexes (including the primary key index).
Add a comment: for which type of queries does an index NOT help (e.g., full table scan patterns)?
Add a comment: what is the trade-off of adding too many indexes?
description.hint: Indexes speed up SELECT WHERE queries but slow down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE slightly, because the index must also be updated. They also consume disk space.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: Carol/Williams/USA row
Q2: 5 USA customers alphabetically by last name
pg_indexes: shows primary key index + 2 custom indexes

description.task: Create a composite index on multiple columns and a unique index, then test how they enforce uniqueness and speed up multi-column searches.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT,
  product_id  INT,
  order_date  DATE,
  status      VARCHAR(20),
  amount      DECIMAL(8,2)
);

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 101, 5, '2026-01-05', 'shipped',    199.99),
  (2, 102, 3, '2026-01-06', 'delivered',  49.99),
  (3, 101, 7, '2026-01-07', 'processing', 99.99),
  (4, 103, 5, '2026-01-08', 'shipped',   299.99),
  (5, 101, 3, '2026-01-09', 'delivered',  49.99),
  (6, 104, 9, '2026-01-10', 'new',        15.00);

-- Composite index: speeds up queries filtering by customer AND date
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_date ON orders(customer_id, order_date);

-- Composite index: speeds up status + date queries for reporting  
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_date ON orders(status, order_date);

-- Query 1: All orders for customer 101 in date range (uses composite index)
SELECT order_id, product_id, order_date, amount
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 101
AND   order_date BETWEEN '2026-01-01' AND '2026-01-31'
ORDER BY order_date;

-- Query 2: All shipped orders this month
SELECT order_id, customer_id, amount FROM orders
WHERE status = 'shipped'
AND   order_date >= '2026-01-01'
ORDER BY order_date;
instructions: Run all CREATE TABLE, INSERT, and CREATE INDEX statements.
Run Query 1 — should return 3 orders for customer 101.
Run Query 2 — should return 2 shipped orders.
In a comment, explain: why does the ORDER of columns in a composite index matter? (customer_id, order_date) vs (order_date, customer_id).
description.hint: A composite index (A, B) helps queries that: filter by A alone, or filter by A AND B. It does NOT help queries that filter by B alone because the index is sorted by A first.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: 3 orders for customer 101
Q2: orders 1 and 4 (both shipped)

title: When NOT to Use an Index

description.task: Analyse scenarios where indexes would help vs. harm and write appropriate SQL for each case.
description.context: Indexes are powerful but not always beneficial. Understanding when to add — and when to avoid — indexes is a critical database design skill.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE large_log (
  log_id      SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  event_type  VARCHAR(20),  -- low cardinality: only 'info','warn','error'
  user_id     INT,
  message     TEXT,
  logged_at   TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO large_log (event_type, user_id, message, logged_at) VALUES
  ('info',  101, 'User logged in',           NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour'),
  ('warn',  102, 'Slow query detected',       NOW() - INTERVAL '2 hours'),
  ('error', 103, 'Connection timeout',        NOW() - INTERVAL '3 hours'),
  ('info',  104, 'File uploaded',             NOW() - INTERVAL '4 hours'),
  ('error', 101, 'Disk space low',            NOW() - INTERVAL '5 hours'),
  ('info',  105, 'Report generated',          NOW() - INTERVAL '6 hours'),
  ('warn',  106, 'High memory usage',         NOW() - INTERVAL '7 hours'),
  ('info',  107, 'Backup completed',          NOW() - INTERVAL '8 hours');

-- GOOD index: high cardinality column (user_id)
CREATE INDEX idx_log_user ON large_log(user_id);

-- GOOD index: timestamp range queries
CREATE INDEX idx_log_time ON large_log(logged_at);

-- BAD idea: low cardinality (only 3 values — index rarely helps)
-- CREATE INDEX idx_log_type ON large_log(event_type); -- usually skipped

-- Query: Good use of index — filter by specific user_id
SELECT event_type, message, logged_at FROM large_log WHERE user_id = 101 ORDER BY logged_at;

-- Query: Good use of index — recent logs within time range
SELECT * FROM large_log WHERE logged_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '5 hours' ORDER BY logged_at;

-- Query: Why event_type index doesn't help much
SELECT COUNT(*) AS info_count FROM large_log WHERE event_type = 'info';
instructions: Run all CREATE, INSERT, and CREATE INDEX statements.
In a comment, explain cardinality: why is a Boolean or status column (few distinct values) a poor index candidate?
List 3 scenarios where you SHOULD add an index and 2 where you should NOT.
Add a comment: what is a covering index and when would it eliminate a table lookup entirely?
description.hint: Low cardinality = few distinct values (like 'info'/'warn'/'error'). When 30% of rows match, a full table scan is often faster than an index scan. High cardinality = user_id — each value narrows down to just a few rows.
description.expectedOutput:
User 101 query: 2 events (login + disk space)
Time range query: last 5 hours events
Info count: 4
